groups.title: Essentials
groups.description: Product context, interface context, user goal, target users, requested UX elements, required facts, task mode, tone, language, and existing copy when relevant.

groups.title: Flow & Behavior
groups.description: User state, next action, supported recovery options, and device or flow variations.

groups.title: Voice & Accessibility
groups.description: Brand voice, required terminology, reading level, and accessibility priorities.

groups.title: Constraints & Delivery
groups.description: Length preference, variant count, prohibited content, sensitive-domain controls, compliance notes, and remaining context.

variables.label: Product or feature
variables.placeholder: Example: Team calendar, payment checkout, password reset
variables.hint: Name the product, feature, or area where the copy will appear.

help.what: Provide the product or feature for this in-app UX copy request.
help.why: The model needs to understand what the interface belongs to and what terminology is appropriate.
help.example: Good example: Team calendar, payment checkout, password reset
help.avoid: Avoid vague product names, internal project codenames, or unsupported feature claims.

variables.label: Screen or interface context
variables.placeholder: Describe the screen, modal, form, notification, or step in the workflow.
variables.hint: Explain where the text appears and what is happening at that moment.

help.what: Provide the screen or interface context for this in-app UX copy request.
help.why: UX copy depends heavily on the exact screen, interaction, and surrounding interface.
help.example: Good example: Password reset form after the user opens a recovery link.
help.avoid: Avoid describing the whole product when the copy is needed for one specific screen, state, or component.

variables.label: User goal
variables.placeholder: Example: Complete payment, recover access, invite a teammate
variables.hint: State what the user is trying to accomplish in this flow.

help.what: Provide the user goal for this in-app UX copy request.
help.why: The intended user outcome determines the wording, hierarchy, and primary action.
help.example: Good example: Recover access to the account
help.avoid: Avoid vague placeholders, unsupported behavior, multiple competing workflows, or product claims that are not confirmed.

variables.label: Target users
variables.placeholder: Example: First-time users with limited technical experience
variables.hint: Describe the people who will read and act on this copy.

help.what: Provide the target users for this in-app UX copy request.
help.why: Audience knowledge affects vocabulary, guidance depth, assumptions, and reading complexity.
help.example: Good example: First-time users with limited technical experience
help.avoid: Avoid vague placeholders, unsupported behavior, multiple competing workflows, or product claims that are not confirmed.

variables.label: UX copy needed
variables.placeholder: Buttons and links, validation messages, error messages
variables.hint: List all interface elements you need.

help.what: Provide the ux copy needed for this in-app UX copy request.
help.why: Different interface elements require different structures, lengths, and information priorities.
help.example: Good example: Buttons and links, validation messages, success states
help.avoid: Avoid asking for unrelated interface elements that are not part of the current screen or workflow.

variables.label: Required facts and details
variables.placeholder: Include product rules, causes, limitations, requirements, and confirmed details.
variables.hint: Provide only factual information that the copy may safely communicate.

help.what: Provide the required facts and details for this in-app UX copy request.
help.why: The model must not invent product behavior, causes, requirements, or resolution details.
help.example: Good example: The reset link expires after 30 minutes. Passwords must be at least 12 characters.
help.avoid: Avoid invented product behavior, unsupported causes, unknown recovery paths, prices, deadlines, or guarantees.

variables.label: Copy task
variables.placeholder: Choose the type of work.
variables.hint: Select whether to create, rewrite, or audit the interface copy.

help.what: Optional context for the copy task field.
help.why: Creating new microcopy and evaluating existing interface text require different workflows.
help.example: Good example: Create new copy
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake urgency, manipulative wording, hidden consequences, sensitive private data, or unsupported compliance claims.

variables.label: Tone
variables.placeholder: Choose the preferred tone.
variables.hint: The tone will be adapted to the situation and severity of the message.

help.what: Optional context for the tone field.
help.why: Tone materially affects onboarding, errors, warnings, and confirmation messages.
help.example: Good example: Clear and supportive
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake urgency, manipulative wording, hidden consequences, sensitive private data, or unsupported compliance claims.

variables.label: Output language
variables.placeholder: Example: English, Spanish, Ukrainian
variables.hint: Specify the language for all generated interface copy.

help.what: Optional context for the output language field.
help.why: Interface length, grammar, terminology, and formality vary by language.
help.example: Good example: English
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake urgency, manipulative wording, hidden consequences, sensitive private data, or unsupported compliance claims.

variables.label: Current interface copy
variables.placeholder: Paste the existing labels, messages, or interface text.
variables.hint: Include the copy that should be rewritten or reviewed.

help.what: Optional context for the current interface copy field.
help.why: The existing wording is necessary when the user requests a rewrite or copy audit.
help.example: Good example: Reset failed. Try again.
help.avoid: Avoid pasting confidential data, passwords, tokens, full payment details, or private user information.

variables.label: User state
variables.placeholder: Choose the user's likely state.
variables.hint: Helps adjust clarity, reassurance, urgency, and information density.

help.what: Optional context for the user state field.
help.why: The user's state changes how much explanation, reassurance, or caution is appropriate.
help.example: Good example: Blocked by an error
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake urgency, manipulative wording, hidden consequences, sensitive private data, or unsupported compliance claims.

variables.label: Expected next action
variables.placeholder: Example: Retry payment, open settings, continue to dashboard
variables.hint: Describe the safest or most useful action after reading the copy.

help.what: Optional context for the expected next action field.
help.why: Good UX copy should guide the user toward a clear and accurate next step.
help.example: Good example: Request a new password reset link
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake urgency, manipulative wording, hidden consequences, sensitive private data, or unsupported compliance claims.

variables.label: Error recovery options
variables.placeholder: Explain what the user can do to fix, retry, or get help.
variables.hint: Include only recovery actions that the product actually supports.

help.what: Optional context for the error recovery options field.
help.why: Error copy should offer a real recovery path rather than a vague failure statement.
help.example: Good example: Users can request a new link or contact support if they no longer have access to the email address.
help.avoid: Avoid recovery steps the product does not actually support, such as retry, contact support, or refresh, unless confirmed.

variables.label: Device or flow variations
variables.placeholder: Example: Mobile modal, desktop sidebar, guest user, paid-plan variation
variables.hint: Describe contexts that require meaningfully different wording.

help.what: Optional context for the device or flow variations field.
help.why: The same action may require different copy across devices, account states, or workflow branches.
help.example: Good example: Mobile modal, desktop full-page form, guest user
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake urgency, manipulative wording, hidden consequences, sensitive private data, or unsupported compliance claims.

variables.label: Brand voice
variables.placeholder: Describe the brand personality or provide a short voice example.
variables.hint: Include useful voice traits, not general writing instructions.

help.what: Optional context for the brand voice field.
help.why: Established products may need interface copy to match an existing verbal identity.
help.example: Good example: Direct, calm, practical, and never overly enthusiastic
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake urgency, manipulative wording, hidden consequences, sensitive private data, or unsupported compliance claims.

variables.label: Required terminology
variables.placeholder: List preferred terms, UI names, capitalization, or words to replace.
variables.hint: Use this for product-specific labels and terminology consistency.

help.what: Optional context for the required terminology field.
help.why: Consistent interface terminology prevents confusion and implementation mismatches.
help.example: Good example: Use sign in, not log in. Use workspace, not account group.
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake urgency, manipulative wording, hidden consequences, sensitive private data, or unsupported compliance claims.

variables.label: Reading level
variables.placeholder: Choose the intended reading level.
variables.hint: Controls vocabulary and sentence complexity without oversimplifying meaning.

help.what: Optional context for the reading level field.
help.why: Some products require simplified language while others use established technical terminology.
help.example: Good example: General audience
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake urgency, manipulative wording, hidden consequences, sensitive private data, or unsupported compliance claims.

variables.label: Accessibility priorities
variables.placeholder: Clear language, screen-reader clarity, avoid color-only references
variables.hint: List relevant accessibility priorities.

help.what: Optional context for the accessibility priorities field.
help.why: These requirements affect labels, instructions, error descriptions, and references to visual controls.
help.example: Good example: Clear language, screen-reader clarity
help.avoid: Avoid claiming WCAG, legal, or formal accessibility compliance unless it has been separately verified.

variables.label: Copy length
variables.placeholder: Choose the preferred level of brevity.
variables.hint: Element-specific clarity takes priority over arbitrary uniform length.

help.what: Optional context for the copy length field.
help.why: Space constraints and user complexity may require different levels of explanation.
help.example: Good example: Concise with context
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake urgency, manipulative wording, hidden consequences, sensitive private data, or unsupported compliance claims.

variables.label: Variants per element
variables.hint: Choose how many usable alternatives to generate for each item.

help.what: Optional context for the variants per element field.
help.why: Some teams need one implementation-ready option while others need alternatives for review.
help.example: Good example: 2
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake urgency, manipulative wording, hidden consequences, sensitive private data, or unsupported compliance claims.

variables.label: Words or claims to avoid
variables.placeholder: List prohibited terms, promises, claims, or sensitive wording.
variables.hint: Combine all product-specific restrictions in this field.

help.what: Optional context for the words or claims to avoid field.
help.why: The user may know legal, brand, product, or localization restrictions that cannot be inferred.
help.example: Good example: Avoid secure, instant, guaranteed, free, and any unsupported recovery promise.
help.avoid: Avoid conflicts with required product terminology unless the conflict is explicitly documented.

variables.label: Sensitive domain
variables.placeholder: Select a regulated or sensitive context.
variables.hint: Used to apply cautious wording and avoid unsupported high-stakes guidance.

help.what: Optional context for the sensitive domain field.
help.why: Sensitive product areas require stricter claim, instruction, privacy, and risk handling.
help.example: Good example: Account and privacy
help.avoid: Avoid invented medical, legal, financial, safety, employment, privacy, or compliance guidance.

variables.label: Compliance requirements
variables.placeholder: Provide approved wording, jurisdiction, warnings, or required disclosures.
variables.hint: Include confirmed requirements only; the model will not invent legal language.

help.what: Optional context for the compliance requirements field.
help.why: Regulated workflows may require exact approved information that only the user can provide.
help.example: Good example: Use approved account-recovery wording and do not expose email addresses in full.
help.avoid: Avoid invented medical, legal, financial, safety, employment, privacy, or compliance guidance.

variables.label: Additional context
variables.placeholder: Add any remaining requirement that materially affects the interface copy.
variables.hint: Do not repeat information already entered in another field.

help.what: Optional context for the additional context field.
help.why: Provides one controlled place for important edge cases without creating miscellaneous fields.
help.example: Good example: Keep button labels short and avoid blame.
help.avoid: Avoid guessed facts, fake urgency, manipulative wording, hidden consequences, sensitive private data, or unsupported compliance claims.
